\title{The Validation Gap: When and Why Offline Metrics Fail to Predict Robot Policy Success}
\author{Eswar Krishnan}
\date{}

\begin{document}
\maketitle

\begin{abstract}
Imitation-learned robot policies are evaluated almost universally by success rate on the
target task, yet during training that quantity is too expensive to compute, so the
deployable checkpoint is chosen by minimising a validation loss against held-out expert
actions. The field has repeatedly noted that this proxy is unreliable, but no work has
systematically characterised when offline metrics succeed, when they fail, or whether any
offline signal recovers what validation loss misses. We treat this as a calibration
question. We train a Diffusion Policy on a suite of manipulation tasks spanning horizons
and contact regimes, save dense checkpoints, and score each checkpoint two ways: a battery
of offline metrics computed from the policy and the demonstrations alone, and full
closed-loop success-rate evaluation. We then ask, per regime, which offline metric tracks
deployment most faithfully and whether a composite predictor transfers to held-out tasks.
The protocol is pre-registered: tasks, metrics, rollout count, and analysis are fixed in
advance, so the contribution stands regardless of which way the data falls. The work needs
no robot hardware and fits on consumer GPUs.
\end{abstract}
